notes.anchor: M. CICERO P. SERVILIO CONLEGAE S.
notes.note: The letter-heading to Cicero's augural colleague (conlega) P. Servilius Vatia Isauricus the younger, proconsul of Asia (46-44 BC); the third of the three letters (13.70-72) commending friends to him in his province.

notes.anchor: The affairs, the debt-claims, and the Asian holdings of Caerellia
notes.note: Caerellia, a wealthy and learned woman friend of Cicero, had business interests in the province of Asia: rem (her general affairs), nomina (outstanding loans/debt-claims registered in account-books), and possessiones (landed property). Such cross-provincial money-lending by Roman investors regularly required a governor's cooperation to enforce.

notes.anchor: that decree of the Senate which was passed concerning the heirs of C. Vennonius
notes.note: A senatus consultum (decree of the Senate) carried only moral, not strictly legal, force, but a conscientious governor would treat it as authoritative; Cicero suggests Servilius could read this particular decree, issued in the matter of C. Vennonius's heirs, as a lever to assist Caerellia's claims.
